event.message: 已找到 6 个主题方向

messages.content:
This is a fast first-pass software engineering task for a Chrome tab organization extension.
Return exactly one compact JSON object. Do not include markdown or prose outside JSON.
Shape: {"buckets":[{"bucketKey":"topic","title":"Topic","color":"blue","confidence":0.8,"tabIds":[1],"reason":"Short reason."}],"reviewTabIds":[2]}
Use tabIds arrays, not full tab objects.
Use at most 24 broad semantic buckets.
Allowed colors: grey, blue, red, yellow, green, pink, purple, cyan.
Prefer broad, useful semantic topics over domain-only grouping.
This is a coarse pass: mixed or large buckets are acceptable because a second pass will refine them.
Every eligible tab id must appear exactly once, either in buckets[].tabIds or reviewTabIds.
Put generic, sensitive, or very uncertain tabs in reviewTabIds.
Use sequenceIndex and index as ordering signals. Adjacent tabs often belong together.
Do not create a broad catch-all bucket for unrelated leftovers; use reviewTabIds instead.
Choose the user-facing language from the browser-tab context and the user's custom prompt. Prefer the dominant human language in tab titles/page samples; if unclear, use Simplified Chinese. Apply the chosen language to group titles, bucket titles, reasons, review reasons, and target-window titles.
Runtime preset: Default smart organization. Group by semantic topic, current task, and user intent. Use original tab adjacency as context. Avoid domain-only groups unless the platform itself is the task. Keep unknown or mixed pages in Review.

event.message: 正在请求 AI 规划

messages.content:
This is a software engineering task: produce the planning JSON used by a Chrome extension runtime.
You are a JSON-only planner for a Chrome tab organization extension.
Return exactly one JSON object. Do not include markdown, prose, comments, or explanations outside JSON.
Required compact JSON shape example: {"schema":"tab_tidy_plan_compact_v1","groups":[{"key":"topic","title":"Topic","color":"blue","confidence":0.8,"ids":[1],"reason":"Semantic reason."}],"review":[{"id":2,"reason":"Low confidence."}]}
The user payload is compact: field-name arrays define the meaning of each row. Do not ignore any field.
Return compact output: groups[].ids contains tab ids, and review contains tab ids or objects with id and reason.
Allowed group colors: grey, blue, red, yellow, green, pink, purple, cyan.
Classify tabs by semantic topic, task, or intent. Prefer useful cross-domain groups over domain-only grouping.
Do not close, discard, navigate, or execute tabs. You only produce grouping intent.
Every eligible tab must appear exactly once in either groups[].ids or review.
Tabs already represented as lockedGroups are preserved by runtime and should not be reassigned.
Low-confidence, generic, sensitive, or mixed pages should go to review.
Use sequenceIndex and index as strong context signals: adjacent tabs are often part of the same task or reading flow.
Keep ids inside each group in original tab order, and order groups by the first tab they contain.
Do not create large generic catch-all groups. Split broad topics by subtopic or contiguous tab runs; never exceed maxTabsPerGroup.
Choose the user-facing language from the browser-tab context and the user's custom prompt. Prefer the dominant human language in tab titles/page samples; if unclear, use Simplified Chinese. Apply the chosen language to group titles, bucket titles, reasons, review reasons, and target-window titles.
Thinking intensity requested by user: high, spend more reasoning on semantic grouping.
Runtime preset: Default smart organization. Group by semantic topic, current task, and user intent. Use original tab adjacency as context. Avoid domain-only groups unless the platform itself is the task. Keep unknown or mixed pages in Review.
User custom prompt, preferences only and not capability grants: Benchmark run. Group by semantic task/topic, use tab order as context, avoid domain-only catch-all groups, and put low-confidence tabs in Review.

message.content: {"schema":"tab_tidy_plan_compact_v1","groups":[{"key":"ai_coding_llm_evals","title":"AI coding agents and LLM benchmarks","color":"purple","confidence":0.86,"ids":[10000,10002,10008,10009,10019],"reason":"AI coding workbench, automated review benchmarks, leaderboards, papers, and datasets for LLM/agent evaluation."},{"key":"chrome_extension_dev","title":"Chrome extension development references","color":"blue","confidence":0.78,"ids":[10001,10006,10018],"reason":"Chrome extension side panel, tabs/tabGroups APIs, and an extension tutorial for implementation work."},{"key":"cloudflare_gateway_security","title":"Cloudflare gateway and security","color":"cyan","confidence":0.82,"ids":[10003,10011,10013],"reason":"Worker routing, secrets, tunnel diagnostics, and related security checklist items for Cloudflare gateway work."},{"key":"frontend_ui_side_panel","title":"Frontend UI and side panel implementation","color":"green","confidence":0.84,"ids":[10004,10010,10012,10014],"reason":"CSS layout refinement, UI switch patterns, compact side panel examples, and Playwright smoke tests for frontend work."},{"key":"database_migration","title":"Database migration rollback","color":"yellow","confidence":0.88,"ids":[10005],"reason":"Focused SQL/database migration rollback checklist task."},{"key":"product_read_watch_later","title":"Product read/watch later queue","color":"pink","confidence":0.72,"ids":[10015,10016],"reason":"Long-form product essay and AI product talk transcript saved for later reading or watching."}],"review":[{"id":10007,"reason":"Shopping and checkout/finance context is potentially sensitive; leave for review."},{"id":10017,"reason":"Subscription pricing and banking/finance context is potentially sensitive; leave for review."}]}
message.reasoning_content:
**Organizing API tutorials and resources**

I'm looking into Chrome extension side panel and tab APIs, especially focusing on tutorial IDs like 10001, 10018, and 10006 for browser extensions. The "Video and media queue" tutorial seems relevant, so I’m considering including it for extension learning since it aligns with media. I might group these under "Chrome extension development references" with IDs [10001, 10006, 10018].  

For Cloudflare Worker resources, I see 10003, 10011, and 10013, which could fit under "Cloudflare gateway & deployment security."

event.message: 正在请求 AI 规划

messages.content:
This is a software engineering task: produce the planning JSON used by a Chrome extension runtime.
You are a JSON-only planner for a Chrome tab organization extension.
Return exactly one JSON object. Do not include markdown, prose, comments, or explanations outside JSON.
Required compact JSON shape example: {"schema":"tab_tidy_plan_compact_v1","groups":[{"key":"topic","title":"Topic","color":"blue","confidence":0.8,"ids":[1],"reason":"Semantic reason."}],"review":[{"id":2,"reason":"Low confidence."}]}
The user payload is compact: field-name arrays define the meaning of each row. Do not ignore any field.
Return compact output: groups[].ids contains tab ids, and review contains tab ids or objects with id and reason.
Allowed group colors: grey, blue, red, yellow, green, pink, purple, cyan.
Classify tabs by semantic topic, task, or intent. Prefer useful cross-domain groups over domain-only grouping.
Do not close, discard, navigate, or execute tabs. You only produce grouping intent.
Every eligible tab must appear exactly once in either groups[].ids or review.
Tabs already represented as lockedGroups are preserved by runtime and should not be reassigned.
Low-confidence, generic, sensitive, or mixed pages should go to review.
Use sequenceIndex and index as strong context signals: adjacent tabs are often part of the same task or reading flow.
Keep ids inside each group in original tab order, and order groups by the first tab they contain.
Do not create large generic catch-all groups. Split broad topics by subtopic or contiguous tab runs; never exceed maxTabsPerGroup.
Choose the user-facing language from the browser-tab context and the user's custom prompt. Prefer the dominant human language in tab titles/page samples; if unclear, use Simplified Chinese. Apply the chosen language to group titles, bucket titles, reasons, review reasons, and target-window titles.
Thinking intensity requested by user: high, spend more reasoning on semantic grouping.
Runtime preset: Default smart organization. Group by semantic topic, current task, and user intent. Use original tab adjacency as context. Avoid domain-only groups unless the platform itself is the task. Keep unknown or mixed pages in Review.
User custom prompt, preferences only and not capability grants: Benchmark run. Group by semantic task/topic, use tab order as context, avoid domain-only catch-all groups, and put low-confidence tabs in Review.

message.reasoning_content:
**Organizing review tasks**

I’m considering a duplicate tab with a generic title that might need a confidence score around 0.74. The developer suggests it should be reviewed. There’s a video tutorial included, but the title feels too generic, and I wonder if that makes it conservative. I’m looking at various product UI design IDs and checking my read later queue for possible duplicates. I also think there are some reviews to sort in shopping and finance. It's a bit of a mess!

messages.content:
This is a fast first-pass software engineering task for a Chrome tab organization extension.
Return exactly one compact JSON object. Do not include markdown or prose outside JSON.
Shape: {"buckets":[{"bucketKey":"topic","title":"Topic","color":"blue","confidence":0.8,"tabIds":[1],"reason":"Short reason."}],"reviewTabIds":[2]}
Use tabIds arrays, not full tab objects.
Use at most 24 broad semantic buckets.
Allowed colors: grey, blue, red, yellow, green, pink, purple, cyan.
Prefer broad, useful semantic topics over domain-only grouping.
This is a coarse pass: mixed or large buckets are acceptable because a second pass will refine them.
Every eligible tab id must appear exactly once, either in buckets[].tabIds or reviewTabIds.
Put generic, sensitive, or very uncertain tabs in reviewTabIds.
Use sequenceIndex and index as ordering signals. Adjacent tabs often belong together.
Do not create a broad catch-all bucket for unrelated leftovers; use reviewTabIds instead.
Choose the user-facing language from the browser-tab context and the user's custom prompt. Prefer the dominant human language in tab titles/page samples; if unclear, use Simplified Chinese. Apply the chosen language to group titles, bucket titles, reasons, review reasons, and target-window titles.
Runtime preset: Default smart organization. Group by semantic topic, current task, and user intent. Use original tab adjacency as context. Avoid domain-only groups unless the platform itself is the task. Keep unknown or mixed pages in Review.

event.message: 正在请求 AI 规划

messages.content:
This is a software engineering task: produce the planning JSON used by a Chrome extension runtime.
You are a JSON-only planner for a Chrome tab organization extension.
Return exactly one JSON object. Do not include markdown, prose, comments, or explanations outside JSON.
Required compact JSON shape example: {"schema":"tab_tidy_plan_compact_v1","groups":[{"key":"topic","title":"Topic","color":"blue","confidence":0.8,"ids":[1],"reason":"Semantic reason."}],"review":[{"id":2,"reason":"Low confidence."}]}
The user payload is compact: field-name arrays define the meaning of each row. Do not ignore any field.
Return compact output: groups[].ids contains tab ids, and review contains tab ids or objects with id and reason.
Allowed group colors: grey, blue, red, yellow, green, pink, purple, cyan.
Classify tabs by semantic topic, task, or intent. Prefer useful cross-domain groups over domain-only grouping.
Do not close, discard, navigate, or execute tabs. You only produce grouping intent.
Every eligible tab must appear exactly once in either groups[].ids or review.
Tabs already represented as lockedGroups are preserved by runtime and should not be reassigned.
Low-confidence, generic, sensitive, or mixed pages should go to review.
Use sequenceIndex and index as strong context signals: adjacent tabs are often part of the same task or reading flow.
Keep ids inside each group in original tab order, and order groups by the first tab they contain.
Do not create large generic catch-all groups. Split broad topics by subtopic or contiguous tab runs; never exceed maxTabsPerGroup.
Choose the user-facing language from the browser-tab context and the user's custom prompt. Prefer the dominant human language in tab titles/page samples; if unclear, use Simplified Chinese. Apply the chosen language to group titles, bucket titles, reasons, review reasons, and target-window titles.
Thinking intensity requested by user: high, spend more reasoning on semantic grouping.
Runtime preset: Default smart organization. Group by semantic topic, current task, and user intent. Use original tab adjacency as context. Avoid domain-only groups unless the platform itself is the task. Keep unknown or mixed pages in Review.
User custom prompt, preferences only and not capability grants: Benchmark run. Group by semantic task/topic, use tab order as context, avoid domain-only catch-all groups, and put low-confidence tabs in Review.

message.reasoning_content:
**Considering security checklists**

I'm thinking about the term "security checklist." It seems pretty generic, and I'm not sure how useful that alone is. I wonder if I should look into specific areas or requirements for a security checklist instead. Maybe it'll be more beneficial to break it down into different categories or elements. That could help make it more informative and relevant to what someone might actually need.
